content:
# Numbers Chapter 32 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 188-190 | **Source Image:** `NUM_032_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_032_page_002.jpg`, `NUM_032_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. pa_0138-pa_0139

---

**1** Now the sons of Reuben and the sons of Gad had very large herds of livestock — vast numbers of them. And when they saw the land of Jazer and the land of Gilead, they realized the region was ideal for livestock.

**2** And the sons of Gad and the sons of Reuben came and spoke to Moses and to Eleazar the priest and to the leaders of the congregation, saying,

**3** "Ataroth, Dibon, Jazer, Nimrah, Heshbon, Elealeh, Sebam, Nebo, and Beon —

**4** the land that YHWH struck down before the congregation of Israel — it is land fit for livestock, and your servants have livestock."

**5** And they said, "If we have found favor in your eyes, let this land be given to your servants as a possession. Do not make us cross over the Jordan."

**6** And Moses said to the sons of Gad and to the sons of Reuben, "Shall your brothers go to war while you sit here?

**7** Why would you discourage the hearts of the sons of Israel from crossing over into the land that YHWH has given them?

**8** This is exactly what your fathers did when I sent them from Kadesh-barnea to scout out the land.

**9** They went up to the Valley of Eshcol and saw the land, and then they crushed the spirit of the sons of Israel so that they refused to enter the land YHWH had given them.

**10** And YHWH's anger blazed that day, and he swore an oath, saying,

**11** 'None of the men who came up out of Egypt, from twenty years old and above, shall see the land I swore to give to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob — because they did not follow me wholeheartedly —

**12** none except Caleb son of Jephunneh the Kenizzite and Joshua son of Nun, for they followed YHWH wholeheartedly.'

**13** And YHWH's anger burned against Israel, and he made them wander in the wilderness for forty years, until the entire generation that had done evil in the sight of YHWH was consumed.

**14** And now look — you have risen up in your fathers' place, a brood of sinful men, to add still more to the burning anger of YHWH against Israel!

**15** For if you turn away from following him, he will once again abandon them in the wilderness, and you will bring destruction on all this people."

**16** And they drew near to him and said, "We will build sheepfolds here for our flocks and towns for our little ones,

**17** but we ourselves will arm up as the vanguard before the sons of Israel until we have brought them to their place. Meanwhile, our little ones will remain in the fortified towns, safe from the inhabitants of the land.

**18** We will not return to our homes until every one of the sons of Israel has received his inheritance.

**19** For we will not inherit with them on the other side of the Jordan and beyond, because our inheritance has come to us on the east side of the Jordan."

**20** And Moses said to them, "If you do this — if you arm yourselves for war before YHWH,

**21** and every armed man among you crosses the Jordan before YHWH until he has driven out his enemies from before him,

**22** and the land is subdued before YHWH — then after that you may return and be free of obligation to YHWH and to Israel. And this land will be yours as a possession before YHWH.

**23** But if you do not do this, you will have sinned against YHWH — and know this: your sin will find you out.

**24** Build towns for your little ones and folds for your flocks, and do what you have promised."

**25** And the sons of Gad and the sons of Reuben said to Moses, "Your servants will do just as my lord commands.

**26** Our children, our wives, our flocks, and all our livestock will remain there in the towns of Gilead.

**27** But your servants, every man armed for battle, will cross over before YHWH to wage war, just as my lord says."

**28** And Moses gave command concerning them to Eleazar the priest and to Joshua son of Nun and to the heads of the ancestral houses of the tribes of the sons of Israel.

**29** And Moses said to them, "If the sons of Gad and the sons of Reuben cross over the Jordan with you — every man armed for war before YHWH — and the land is subdued before you, then you shall give them the land of Gilead as a possession.

**30** But if they do not cross over with you armed for battle, they shall receive a possession among you in the land of Canaan."

**31** And the sons of Gad and the sons of Reuben answered, saying, "What YHWH has spoken to your servants, that we will do.

**32** We ourselves will cross over armed before YHWH into the land of Canaan, and our inherited possession will remain with us on this side of the Jordan."

**33** And Moses gave to them — to the sons of Gad and to the sons of Reuben and to the half-tribe of Manasseh son of Joseph — the kingdom of Sihon king of the Amorites and the kingdom of Og king of Bashan, the land with its cities and territories, the towns of the surrounding land.

**34** And the sons of Gad built Dibon, Ataroth, Aroer,

**35** Atroth-shophan, Jazer, Jogbehah,

**36** Beth-nimrah, and Beth-haran — fortified towns and sheepfolds.

**37** And the sons of Reuben built Heshbon, Elealeh, Kiriathaim,

**38** Nebo, Baal-meon — their names changed — and Sibmah. And they gave new names to the cities they rebuilt.

**39** And the sons of Machir son of Manasseh went to Gilead and captured it, and they dispossessed the Amorites who were there.

**40** And Moses gave Gilead to Machir son of Manasseh, and he settled there.

**41** And Jair son of Manasseh went and captured their tent-villages, and he called them Havvoth-jair.

**42** And Nobah went and captured Kenath and its surrounding settlements, and he called it Nobah, after his own name.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v. 1 — "very large herds of livestock":** The Hebrew מִקְנֶה רַב עָצוּם (miqneh rav atsum) doubles the intensifiers ("abundant" and "mighty"), underscoring that the sheer scale of their herds drives the entire negotiation. This is not casual wealth — it is the economic engine behind a bold political request.

**v. 5 — "Do not make us cross over the Jordan":** Hebrew אַל־תַּעֲבִרֵנוּ (al-ta'avirenu). The causative form is key: they are not merely asking to stay — they are asking Moses not to force them across. The verb already frames the Jordan as a boundary of destiny.

**v. 6 — "Shall your brothers go to war while you sit here?":** Moses' rebuke is sharp. The Hebrew הַאַחֵיכֶם יָבֹאוּ לַמִּלְחָמָה וְאַתֶּם תֵּשְׁבוּ פֹה (ha-akheikhem yavo'u la-milkhamah ve-attem teshvu foh) uses a rhetorical question that bites with accusation. The contrast between "go to war" and "sit here" exposes the moral danger of their request.

**v. 9 — "crushed the spirit":** Hebrew וַיָּנִיאוּ (vayyani'u) from the root נוא, meaning to hinder, frustrate, or discourage. The spies did not merely report bad news; they actively broke the people's will. Moses draws a direct line between the old failure and the current request.

**v. 14 — "a brood of sinful men":** Hebrew תַּרְבּוּת אֲנָשִׁים חַטָּאִים (tarbut anashim khatta'im). The word תַּרְבּוּת (tarbut) means "increase, offspring, brood" — Moses is saying they are a new crop of the same sinful seed, multiplying the provocation against YHWH.

**v. 15 — "your sin will find you out":** Hebrew וּמְצָאֶת חַטַּאתְכֶם (u-metza'et khatta'tkhem) from v. 23. This is one of the most memorable lines in the Torah. Sin is personified as a tracker, a pursuer — it does not disappear because you ignore it. The theological weight is enormous: consequences are built into the fabric of moral reality.

**v. 17 — "arm up as the vanguard":** Hebrew חֻשִׁים (khushim), meaning "hurrying, rushing to arms." The Vaticanus reads προφυλακη (prophylake, "advance guard"), confirming the sense that they pledged to go first into danger, not merely to accompany the other tribes.

**v. 22 — "free of obligation":** Hebrew נְקִיִּם (neqi'im), "clean, innocent, free from guilt." The legal weight is important: this is release from a covenant obligation. Only after fulfillment can they return without being held liable before both YHWH and Israel.

**v. 33 — The half-tribe of Manasseh:** Manasseh appears here for the first time in this narrative, having not been part of the original petition. This sudden inclusion has puzzled commentators. It may reflect a separate military reality — the Machirite clan had already begun settling Gilead (vv. 39-42) — or an editorial note integrating the Transjordanian allocation traditions.

**v. 38 — "their names changed":** Hebrew מוּסַבֹּת שֵׁם (musabbot shem), literally "turned around with respect to name." The Israelites renamed Baal-meon and possibly Nebo because these names contained references to Moabite and Canaanite deities. The act of renaming is an act of theological reclamation — claiming the land for YHWH by stripping it of pagan identity.

**v. 41 — Havvoth-jair:** Hebrew חַוֹּת יָאִיר (Khavvot Ya'ir), meaning "the tent-villages of Jair." The word חַוֹּת (khavvot) refers to small, unwalled settlements — clusters of tents or huts rather than fortified towns. This detail shows how settlement in Transjordan was incremental, ranging from fortified cities (v. 36) to pastoral encampments.

**Narrative structure note:** The chapter moves through three dramatic phases: (1) the petition and Moses' furious comparison to the spy generation (vv. 1-15); (2) the counter-offer and Moses' conditional agreement (vv. 16-27); and (3) the formal legal arrangement and the settlement record (vv. 28-42). The tension between tribal self-interest and collective responsibility is the theological backbone of the narrative — a question that echoes throughout Israel's history.
